---
title: "Annotated voxtral.c"
---

## Scope and provenance

`Sources/CVoxtralEngine/Engine/voxtral.c` is derived from antirez's
[`voxtral.c`](https://github.com/antirez/voxtral.c) at commit
`134d366c24d20c64b614a3dcc8bda2a6922d077d`. The license and import record are
preserved under `Vendor/VoxtralEngine`.

This chapter is a line-range commentary over the complete 1,652-line file.
Generated shader source and mathematical kernels live in neighboring files and
are described where called. Use the rendered HTML beside the source file: each
subsection states the exact source range and explains every logical statement
in that range.

## Lines 1-25: translation unit setup

Lines 1-6 state the orchestration sequence. Lines 8-15 import the model,
kernels, safetensors, audio, tokenizer, and optional Metal APIs. Lines 16-21 are
the C runtime and timing dependencies.

```c
int vox_verbose = 0;
int vox_monitor = 0;
```

Lines 23-25 define process-global diagnostics. They are not per model or
thread-safe configuration. ClassroomCaptions leaves them quiet and exposes
structured counters instead.

## Lines 27-80: delay conditioning

### `vox_compute_time_embedding`, lines 31-45

`dim` is 3072 and `half` is 1536. For each frequency:

```c
inv_freq = exp(-log(10000) * i / half);
emb = t_value * inv_freq;
out[i] = cos(emb);
out[i + half] = sin(emb);
```

The first half stores cosines and the second half sines. `t_value` is the integer
delay measured in 80 ms tokens, represented as `float`.

### `vox_update_time_conditioning`, lines 47-80

Line 48 accepts a nullable context. Line 49 refreshes the 3072-value time
embedding whenever delay changes.

Lines 51-55 allocate one 3072-value scale per decoder layer:

\[
26 \times 3072 \times 4 \approx 312\text{ KiB}
\]

For every layer, lines 62-67 implement the first dense projection
\(32 \times 3072\). Line 69 applies GELU. Lines 71-78 implement the
\(3072 \times 32\) projection. The result is cached because recomputing it for
every token would waste CPU time and the delay is normally constant.

Allocation failure leaves `ada_scale == NULL`; downstream decoder code must
tolerate this. The project bridge reports model load failure only when the
larger model construction fails, so this small allocation remains an upstream
soft-failure behavior.

## Lines 82-110: adapter weight lookup

Lines 86-87 declare loaders implemented in `voxtral_encoder.c` and
`voxtral_decoder.c`.

`load_bf16_direct` finds a named safetensors tensor and returns a pointer into
the mapped BF16 file. No 8.3 GB copy is made. A missing name is printed and
returns `NULL`.

`vox_adapter_load` resolves the two exact checkpoint keys for the
audio-language projection. Both are required.

## Lines 112-256: model loading

### Context initialization, lines 116-138

`calloc` zeroes all ownership fields, making partial cleanup possible.
`model_dir` is copied into a fixed 512-byte field. The defaults are:

- four delay tokens, 320 ms;
- direct BF16 mapping;
- FP32 KV until the Metal choice is made.

The safetensors path is assembled in a 1024-byte stack buffer. The file remains
open and mapped for the entire model lifetime because many weight pointers
refer directly into it.

### Component loading, lines 140-161

Encoder, adapter, and decoder load in that order. Every failure calls
`vox_free(ctx)`, relying on the zeroed context and null-safe cleanup. Delay
conditioning is computed only after decoder layer weights exist.

### Metal warmup, lines 163-250

The warmup is a latency optimization and a memory policy:

1. choose FP16 decoder KV by default, overridable with
   `VOX_DECODER_KV_FP16=0`;
2. convert/cache merged encoder QKV and SwiGLU `w1+w3`;
3. cache encoder output and down-projection weights;
4. cache adapter weights;
5. cache all decoder projections;
6. cache the tied token embedding/output matrix;
7. prepare merged decoder buffers;
8. warm decoder MPS operations;
9. preallocate decoder and encoder KV caches.

Merged buffers reduce dispatches and repeated concatenation. The dimensions in
lines 176-206 are computed from constants rather than checkpoint metadata; the
runtime is intentionally specialized to this model architecture.

The preallocation sizes exceed the nominal windows slightly to reduce growth
and permit rolling compaction.

## Lines 258-349: model destruction

`vox_free` is the inverse ownership walk.

The `FREE0` macro frees only arrays copied or converted to FP32 and nulls the
field. BF16 weight pointers are not individually freed because they point into
the safetensors mapping.

Lines 297-317 distinguish Metal shared allocations from ordinary heap
allocations. Lines 318-342 release conditioning and persistent scratch.
Lines 344-346 close the mapping last, after all borrowed weight pointers are no
longer used. Line 348 releases the context itself.

This ordering is the C ownership graph:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    Ctx[vox_ctx] --> Map[safetensors mapping]
    Ctx --> CPU[owned FP32 arrays]
    Ctx --> KV[Metal/shared KV buffers]
    Ctx --> Scratch[persistent scratch]
    Map --> Borrowed[borrowed BF16 pointers]
    CPU --> Free1[free]
    KV --> Free2[metal_shared_free]
    Scratch --> Free3[free]
    Borrowed --> Close[invalidated by safetensors_close]
```

## Lines 351-407: constants and helpers

The special token IDs are model-specific. Tokens below 1000 are treated as
control tokens. Token 1000 is a raw zero byte in Tekken and needs special
handling later.

`RAW_AUDIO_LENGTH_PER_TOK = 1280` equals 80 ms at 16 kHz.

The first encoder run waits for 312 Mel frames. Later runs use the configurable
interval. Recovery limits bound KV growth, control-token loops, and stalled
decoding.

`trim_ascii_whitespace` edits an owned C string in place.

`tok_embed_bf16_to_f32` converts one embedding row by placing each BF16 word in
the high 16 bits of an IEEE-754 float. This is exact BF16 expansion, not a
numeric multiply.

## Lines 413-479: `struct vox_stream`

The stream is the complete incremental state machine. Its fields fall into ten
groups:

| Lines | State |
| --- | --- |
| 414-415 | shared model context and owned tokenizer |
| 417-420 | incremental Mel frontend and real-audio count |
| 421-433 | convolution and downsampling boundary residuals |
| 435-439 | compactable adapter embedding buffer |
| 441-453 | decoder cursor, previous token, watchdogs, and mode |
| 455-459 | circular output queue |
| 461-463 | optional alternative-token policy |
| 465-468 | decoder scratch |
| 470-471 | encoder batching threshold |
| 473-478 | performance and visible-token counters |

`real_samples_fed` excludes right-padding silence. Watchdogs therefore measure
progress against actual source audio.

`total_adapter` and `gen_pos` are logical positions. `adapter_pos_offset`
records how much physical prefix has been compacted away.

## Lines 481-524: token classification and queue

`stream_classify_token` distinguishes:

- EOS;
- control IDs below 1000;
- text-range IDs that decode to empty/invalid C strings;
- visible text.

This distinction fixed a live failure mode where token 1000 looked like decode
progress but produced no visible bytes.

`stream_enqueue_token` is a circular buffer with one deliberately unused slot.
When full, capacity doubles. Existing elements are copied in logical order,
then head becomes zero and tail becomes the old count.

The queue stores tokenizer-owned `const char *` pointers. They remain valid
until the tokenizer is freed. The project bridge copies bytes before Swift sees
them.

## Lines 526-716: incremental convolution stem

This is the most boundary-sensitive code in the file.

### First convolution, lines 548-611

On the first chunk, Mel is transposed from row-major `[frame, mel]` to the
column-major layout expected by `vox_causal_conv1d`. Causal convolution and
GELU produce one 1280-vector per Mel frame. The last two Mel frames are saved.

On later chunks, the saved two frames are prepended. After convolution, the
first two outputs are discarded because they belong to overlap and include
start-padding assumptions. The tail is then updated.

### Stride alignment, lines 613-658

Conv1 has stride two. Feeding an odd number would cause a padded final output and
shift every future chunk's phase. The code combines a previous one-frame
residual with new conv0 output, sends only an even count, and saves a final odd
frame.

This is equivalent to maintaining:

\[
r' = (r + n) \bmod 2
\]

where \(r\) is the previous residual count and \(n\) is the new conv0 count.

### Second convolution, lines 660-715

The first chunk legitimately uses zero left-padding. Later chunks prepend the
last two conv0 frames and discard the first overlapping conv1 output.

The result is transposed back to row-major `[position, 1280]`, which is the
encoder transformer's input layout.

Every returned allocation belongs to the caller. Several allocation results in
this upstream code are assumed non-null; this is one reason the engine is kept
behind the project bridge and subjected to controlled model/machine
requirements.

## Lines 718-781: compaction and resets

`stream_adapter_compact` removes adapter vectors below `gen_pos`. `memmove` is
required because source and destination overlap.

`stream_reset_decoder_state` clears:

- decoder KV logical length;
- adapter backlog;
- generated position;
- previous token and EOS;
- decoder watchdog counters.

It intentionally keeps Mel and encoder state.

`stream_reset_full_state` creates a fresh Mel context before freeing the old
one, so allocation failure does not destroy the usable stream. It clears
convolution residuals, encoder KV, and then decoder state.

## Lines 783-908: incremental encoder and adapter

`stream_run_encoder` performs four stages.

1. Compute how many undispatched Mel frames exist.
2. Run the incremental convolution stem.
3. Run the 32-layer encoder using its rolling KV cache.
4. combine residual encoder positions into groups divisible by four and run the
   adapter.

The first call requires 312 Mel frames; later calls require `min_new_mel`.
Finishing bypasses the threshold.

Adapter capacity doubles geometrically. The physical write index is:

```c
phys_len = total_adapter - adapter_pos_offset;
```

The function saves zero to three leftover encoder vectors, updates timing, and
discards Mel frames older than `mel_cursor`.

## Lines 910-967: optional alternatives

The application does not use alternatives, but the runtime can:

1. perform numerically stable softmax by subtracting the maximum logit;
2. repeatedly scan for the next-best visible token;
3. accept it when
   \(1 - p_i/p_{best} \leq cutoff\);
4. store up to four alternatives.

This mutates the logits buffer from logits to probabilities, which is safe only
after the best token has already been selected.

## Lines 969-1189: decoder core

### Prompt prefill, lines 973-1047

`prompt_len = 1 + 32 + delay_tokens`. The decoder waits until adapter capacity
covers this prompt.

For each prompt position, the code expands the BOS or streaming-pad embedding
and adds the matching adapter vector. It clears logical KV state, prefills all
but the last position, and uses the last position for the first sampled token.

The token is queued only when classification says visible text. Counters track
whether a restart emitted anything.

### Incremental generation, lines 1049-1133

For every available adapter position:

```c
step_embed = adapter[gen_pos] + embedding(prev_token);
prev_token = decoder_forward(step_embed, logits);
gen_pos++;
```

Visible pieces enter the queue. Control and invalid pieces increment the
non-text watchdog. EOS ends the loop. Timing separates initial prefill from
single-token decode.

The monitor symbols are stderr diagnostics only; ClassroomCaptions does not
parse them.

### Recovery, lines 1135-1188

After compacting consumed adapter vectors, continuous mode chooses a restart
reason. EOS alone may use a decoder-only reset. KV overflow, non-text loops, and
stalls use a full reset; repeated EOS segments with no text also escalate.

If full Mel reinitialization fails, the code falls back to a decoder reset so
the live service remains available.

## Lines 1191-1236: stream construction

The stream:

- borrows `ctx`;
- owns a Tekken tokenizer;
- creates a Mel context with 32 audio-token equivalents of left silence;
- allocates a 256-position token queue;
- allocates logits and two 3072-float scratch vectors;
- resets encoder KV;
- defaults later encoder runs to two seconds.

The app immediately overrides the processing interval to one second and enables
continuous mode through the project bridge.

## Lines 1238-1311: feed and retrieval

`vox_stream_feed` is intentionally small: append PCM to Mel state, count real
samples, run encoder, run decoder.

`vox_stream_finish` first uses the same padding operation as flush, marks the
stream finished, finalizes the Mel frontend, and runs one final encoder/decoder
pass.

`vox_stream_get` dequeues best-token pointers. The diagnostics accessors return
visible token count and accumulated decoder milliseconds. Alternative retrieval
copies a fixed number of pointers per token position.

## Lines 1313-1342: stream destruction

The stream must be destroyed before its model. It prints optional statistics,
then releases Mel, tokenizer, adapter buffer, token queue, scratch, and all
boundary residuals.

## Lines 1344-1596: convenience paths

These paths support the upstream CLI and benchmark:

- `vox_transcribe_audio`: feed one float array and concatenate pieces;
- `vox_transcribe_stdin`: detect WAV versus raw signed 16-bit input;
- `vox_transcribe`: load a WAV path then call the audio helper.

The WAV parser walks RIFF chunks, handles mono or channel averaging, and uses
linear interpolation when resampling. ClassroomCaptions does not use this
resampler for live capture; AVAudioConverter supplies the model format before
the C bridge.

String buffers grow geometrically. Returned strings are caller-owned.

## Lines 1598-1652: live controls

### Flush

Flush calculates:

1. padding to the next 1280-sample boundary;
2. `delay_tokens + 1`;
3. ten extra offline streaming buffer tokens.

It feeds zero samples directly to the Mel context, temporarily lowers the
encoder threshold to one frame, and runs encoder/decoder. It does not increment
`real_samples_fed`, so watchdog time remains tied to real audio.

### Setters

Processing seconds are converted to Mel frames at 100 fps and clamped to at
least one.

Continuous mode is a Boolean.

Maximum decoder context is clamped to `[250, 8000]`.

Delay is clamped to `[80, 2400]` ms, truncated to 80 ms units, and immediately
recomputes adaptive time conditioning.

## Project bridge: why direct Swift calls are avoided

`ClassroomVoxtral.c` adds a narrower contract:

- validates the three model files;
- initializes Metal explicitly and rejects CPU fallback;
- converts PCM16 to reusable float storage;
- exposes structured status codes;
- preserves an unread token across `BUFFER_TOO_SMALL`;
- copies token bytes into caller-owned memory;
- exposes finish, flush, cancellation, counters, and pinned revision.

The bridge's two opaque handles encode ownership:

```c
struct ccv_model {
    vox_ctx_t *engine;
};

struct ccv_stream {
    ccv_model_t *model;       /* borrowed */
    vox_stream_t *engine;     /* owned */
    float *conversion_buffer; /* owned */
    const char *pending_text; /* borrowed tokenizer piece */
    atomic_bool cancelled;
    int finished;
};
```

Swift destroys `ccv_stream_t` before `ccv_model_t`, and all calls for one stream
run on one serial inference queue.

## Complete line map

| Lines | Purpose |
| ---: | --- |
| 1-25 | includes and diagnostics globals |
| 27-80 | delay embedding and adaptive scales |
| 82-110 | encoder/decoder declarations and adapter load |
| 112-256 | model load and Metal warmup |
| 258-349 | model cleanup |
| 351-407 | token, timing, recovery constants and helpers |
| 413-479 | complete streaming state |
| 481-524 | token classes and output queue |
| 526-716 | chunk-correct causal convolution stem |
| 718-781 | adapter compaction and resets |
| 783-908 | incremental encoder and adapter |
| 910-967 | alternative token probabilities |
| 969-1189 | prompt, decode loop, watchdogs, restart |
| 1191-1236 | stream allocation |
| 1238-1311 | feed, finish, counters, token retrieval |
| 1313-1342 | stream cleanup |
| 1344-1596 | offline/stdin convenience APIs |
| 1598-1652 | flush and live configuration |
